body: You need to use the Vary header to indicate to the cache (any CDN) that there are multiple variations of the page.
author: lukebussey

body: Sorry but I don't see how that would help. The data flow is like this Azure FrontDoor CDN -> Our Frontend. If the CDN already cached the page, how would A/B testing work?
author: zmarty

body:
Every CDN has a cache key. Usually it'll be the full URL including query string, but if you serve different content for the same full URL as in the case with a server side a/b test, then you'll need to tell the CDN which headers to also include in the cache key.

A good example of this is when your origin server performs content negotiation to determine if the client can accept gzipped responses. In this case there will be two objects stored in cache for the same URL. One uncompressed, and one compressed. In this case the CDN is using the Content-encoding header as part of it's cache key.

- https://developers.cloudflare.com/cache/about/cache-keys
- https://www.fastly.com/blog/ab-testing-edge#keeping-different-versions-cached-with-http-vary
author: lukebussey

body: How would one do an A/B test using the `Vary` header? I mean, you would need to have something that is sent from the browser on each request and can be unique for a group of users. As far as I can see, only a cookie can do this, and using `Vary: Cookie` does not really work. You can of course add some logic to your CDN to read from the cookie and use that to create a cache key.
author: stigkj

body:
Yeah but what would set the cookie in the first place? Anyway I don't think using Vary header would work.

Our ask is this: If we use middleware to switch between two static pages, then let us override the cache header in next.config.js instead of forcing it like it does for all static pages. So then we would make this route return no-cache, middleware will run on every request, and CDN will not cache this route. Then A/B testing will work. Then when A/B test is done, we will remove the middleware, make one of the two static pages be the official one, and re-enable the caching header.
author: zmarty

body:
Yes you could prevent the a/b tested page from caching but the `no-cache` Cache-control directive doesn't prevent caching. It tells the browser and cdn to revalidate.

`Cache-control: private, no-store` will prevent caching on a CDN

Alternatively if the CDN obeys the Vary header, then you could try something like this in the middleware:

```
X-Variation: <variation name>
Vary: X-Variation
```

@rsmelo92 Yeah getServersideProps doesn't work because loadQuery just kicks off a network request and lets you use that network request later. So it needs to run on the client, as early as possible; not on the server

Separate from this its possible to fully execute the query on the server, and serialize that to the client, but that's a pretty different feature which fully blocks your page

body:
I managed to work around it by adding the formatjs eslint plugin. It can autofix the ID's and if added, there is no need for te plugin (at this point).

source: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/30174#discussioncomment-1922431
author: joggienl

body:
Thanks, it wasn't immediately clear to me that this was possible based on the docs.

It also wasn't immediately clear how it works, so I hope this helps as a quick resource to someone in the same boat:
Once installed, to get autofix working you'll need to specify in your eslint config the interpolation pattern that would have otherwise been in your .babelrc like so:
```
    "formatjs/enforce-id": [
      "error",
      {
        "idInterpolationPattern": "[sha512:contenthash:base64:6]"
      }
    ],
```

author: MDmubarak786

body:
From [Vercel's documentation](https://vercel.com/docs/edge-network/caching#static-files-caching)

> [Static Files Caching](https://vercel.com/docs/edge-network/caching#static-files-caching)
Static files are automatically cached at the edge after the first request and are cached for up to 31 days on Vercel's Edge Network.
If a static file is unchanged, the cached value can persist across deployments due to the hash used in the filename
Optimized images cached will [persist between deployments](https://vercel.com/docs/image-optimization#caching)
The default Cache-Control header contains public, max-age=0, must-revalidate to prevent clients (e.g. browsers) from caching the file locally
[Browser](https://vercel.com/docs/edge-network/caching#browser)
max-age=N, public
max-age=N, immutable
Where N is the number of seconds the response should be cached. The response must also meet the [caching criteria](https://vercel.com/docs/edge-network/caching#how-to-cache-responses).

This ensures that the Caching happens on Vercel's Edge Network (which may be seen in this case as a CDN) and not on the browser.

However it would be great if we could customise this behavior further for those files that do not change at all (and could be cached on the browser)
author: nicolopadovan

body:
@nicolopadovan https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/optimizing/static-assets specifically says it doesn't.

Bit of a terminology issue but I believe the link on "static files" does specifically mean files as opposed to the public folder assets.

Looks like your answer is the only way (if we don't use vercel/etc), cause from the [next.js docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/building-your-application/optimizing/static-assets#caching):
> Next.js cannot safely cache assets in the **public** folder because they may change. The default caching headers applied are:
> Cache-Control: public, max-age=0

body:
## Custom Solution

I decided to host my static files on an external blob storage (like S3), configure it behind a CDN (such as CloudFront), and set up custom caching rules there. Although the initial setup was somewhat frustrating, this approach allows for modifications—such as updating images, localization files, and configuration data—without the need to redeploy the entire application. While this setup makes sense for large projects like mine, it might be an unnecessary extra cost & effort for smaller applications.

body: Figured it out, had to with how Github pages work. Basically need to add a `.nojekyll` file in the `out` folder to tell Git to serve the _next files. See https://stackoverflow.com/questions/61450307/js-and-css-not-loading-when-hosting-next-application-on-github-pages
author: bmarshall511

body:
For those who run `npm run deploy` directly instead of using yml file, here is the fix:
```js
"scripts": {
    "dev": "next dev",
    "build": "next build",
    "start": "next start",
    "export": "next export",
    "deploy": "npm run build && npm run export && touch ./out/.nojekyll && gh-pages -d out -t true"
  },
```
This will copy the .nojekyll file to the root folder and serve the static files.
